name: Lumen
short_description: An interactive tabletop display whose grid of light guides rises and changes color via shape-memory-alloy actuators, presenting calm, organic moving shapes for ambient computing.
long_description: Lumen, presented at SIGGRAPH 2004 Emerging Technologies by Ivan Poupyrev and colleagues at Sony, is an electromechanical display made of a two-dimensional array of movable light guides whose height and color can be controlled independently. Each guide is raised and lowered by a shape-memory-alloy (SMA) string that contracts when heated by current, producing smooth, noiseless, organic motion, while a capacitive SmartSkin sensor embedded in the surface recognizes hand shapes and finger motions for direct touch interaction. As an early shape-changing / actuated tangible display, it matters to physical AI and interaction design because it treats physical form itself as an expressive, ambient information medium rather than a screen, foreshadowing later shape displays and 'calm computing' surfaces.
ai_note: Input is capacitive SmartSkin touch sensing and SMA actuation; the paper explicitly contrasts this with camera techniques, and no machine learning is used.

images.caption: A hand interacting with the Lumen display, whose grid of light guides rises to form an illuminated shape.
images.alt_text: A hand touching a small square tabletop display whose array of glowing pixel rods rises at different heights to form a raised, illuminated shape.
